data.title: APA Corporation: Suriname's Promise Vs. Oil Risks - Value Trap Ahead?
data.description:
APA Corporation (NASDAQ:APA) is an independent energy company with a diverse portfolio of offshore and onshore assets across the United States, Egypt, and the North Sea. The company also holds several untapped and exploratory assets, particularly in Suriname, Alaska, and Uruguay.

APA’s business model revolves around the exploration and production of crude oil, which accounts for 70% of its revenue, natural gas (~20%), and natural gas liquids (~10%).

Over the last year, the company has been actively reshuffling its asset portfolio. This includes the divestiture of approximately $950 million in non-core permian assets and a partnership with Total Energies in Suriname. These moves are aimed at optimizing the company’s portfolio by focusing on higher-margin assets.

I came across this stock while screening low P/E ratio companies in the S&P 500. Currently, the company is trading at a forward P/E ratio of ~5.8, presenting an attractive risk-reward profile at the outset.

In this article, I take a deep dive into whether the company is an attractive value play or a value trap by analyzing each of its assets and assessing the associated risks and rewards with them.

Current asset profile

APA was founded in the 1950s as Apache Oil Corporation and has transitioned from a small U.S.-based driller to an international E&P player, now included in the S&P 500. Over the past several decades, the company has successfully navigated the cyclical nature of the oil industry while strategically increasing its production. In the 1990s, APA expanded into international markets through an acquisition in Egypt, and in 2003, it entered the North Sea market.

To understand APA, it is crucial to examine its business model and asset base. The company derives ~70% of its BoE equivalent production from its U.S. assets, primarily in the Permian Basin, where it has around 4,000+ drilling locations. The company’s breakeven price in this region is estimated at ~$45 per barrel WTI. Next, about 20% of its production comes from Egypt, where it benefits from long-term government concessions in the Western Desert and Mediterranean Sea. This region has historically been highly profitable for APA, allowing it to realize above-average returns vs US assets due to favourable gas pricing. Lastly, ~10% of the output comes from the North sea region. However, the company is facing high regulatory costs in the region and is planning to ramp down operations here by 2029.

U.S. Permian Basin

As we can see by the production estimates shared by the company for 2025, it seems that the outlook for 2025 in terms of production output is very similar to last year. Given stable output, the total revenues and hence free cash flow that is generated by the company in this region is largely dependent on the oil and gas prices in the Permian Basin.

At this point, our analysis of the revenues & free cash flows becomes very challenging and would largely depend on the macroeconomic view individual investors may have about oil and gas prices in 2025. Being a very cautious and prudent investor in this regard, I would like to go ahead with the assumption that oil and gas prices continue to stay subdued this year.

I believe in this hypothesis because any geopolitical development which eases the sanctions on Russia or Iran could also lead to a supply glut in the market. Moreover, the current production profile for Permian-related assets leads to a breakeven cost of approximately $40-60 per barrel in the region- depending on the age of asset. Compare that to the just $10 per barrel cost that a country like Saudi Arabia has. Currently OPEC has been holding back on supply during the last year but is finding it increasingly difficult to contain individual member countries. In this situation, I believe it is only a matter of time before Saudi Arabia unleashes an ocean of supply into the market.

This, coupled with a slower-than-expected recovery in China and a “Drill, Baby, Drill” policy under Trump increasing the supply of crude further in the near future, this is the perfect catalyst for increasing supply and reducing demand.

While bulls may argue that the demand for power due to artificial intelligence is going to be insatiable and will lead to a strong increase in dependency on fossil fuels such as natural gas, I believe that cycle will play out over a slightly longer time and may not materialize, at least in 2025. I say this because large tech companies would first prefer to tie up with cleaner power providers such as nuclear or renewable companies, and natural gas is expected to be largely used for grid balancing and peak power management. Moreover, the entire hypothesis of higher power demand also suddenly came into question with the Deepseek breakthrough.

Hence, in my opinion, we should ideally anticipate some reduction in revenues & operational FCF generated from the US-based assets compared to current levels - even if the production remains stable.

International Operations

Within its international operations, APA derives revenue primarily from two key assets—its profitable Egypt business and the challenging waters of the North Sea.

As far as the Egypt business is concerned, I see some stability going forward. The production output is expected to remain consistent and pricing is also likely to stay high. As per the company:

Subsequent to September 30, 2024, but prior to the date of this filing, the Company entered into a new pricing agreement for incremental gas volumes produced in Egypt, making gas exploration and development more economically competitive with oil development.

My understanding is that the Egyptian General Petroleum Corporation is likely to give APA some fixed commitments/ prices, to increase gas output, due to the increased gas demand in the country for power generation. The gas is consumed by utilities in Egypt for power generation, and can be purchased at fixed longer term contracts due to the relatively stable nature of electricity prices.

However, it is noteworthy to mention that the majority of the business done by APA in Egypt is with the government or government entities, and the country is facing extreme economic hardships. The country’s currency has been devalued multiple times last year, and the revenues from Egypt should ideally command a higher country risk premium. The company faced some challenges with receivables from the EG government, as cited in the 10-Q report:

In the past year, the Company’s receivable balancefrom the Egyptian General Petroleum Corporation (EGPC) has gradually increased as payments for the Company’s Egyptian oil and gas sales have been delayed for periods longer than historically experienced. The Company is actively engaged in discussions with the Government of Egypt to resolve the delay in EGPC payments. The Company has received payments throughout the period, and management believes that the Company will be able to collect the total balance of its receivables from this customer.

As far as the North Sea operations are concerned, management has forecasted a ~20% reduction in output from this region. However this should be offset by minor increases in other assets, as north sea contributes only ~10% to the production output of APA. Moreover, by 2029 APA expects to exit from this asset, which should be reflected in the revenues going ahead.

To conclude, I believe that in 2025, despite fluctuations in oil and gas prices, Revenue & Cash flow generated from APA’s international operations should remain largely in line with 2024 levels due to the relatively attractive nature of Egyptian contracts and the relatively small share of income from the North Sea.

Elephant in the room - Suriname

With the core business largely stable and linked to oil prices, it becomes critical to assess the optional valuation of the Suriname asset and the likely capital investment until production to ascertain the fair value of APA.

As published in multiple analyst and news articles, the Suriname asset is being developed by APA in partnership with Total with a 50-50% share in the proceeds of the asset. The asset is expected to generate around 220,000 barrels per day of output and is likely to go live by H1 2028.

The market was extremely worried that this large capital heavy asset may be a drain on the free cash flow of the company until development. The total project is currently estimated to have a capex of $10.5bn. However, as we have seen with multiple E&P projects over the last decade, costs and timeline only go up. From a cost perspective, the current agreement between Total and APA is that 87.5% of the cost up to the first $10 billion is expected to be borne by Total and the remaining 12.5% is expected to be borne by APA. For the next $5 billion, the ratio skews a bit and APA has the increased liability of ~25% of the total cost. For all costs above $15 billion, the ratio further increases and APA is expected to bear ~37.5% of the capex.

Assuming no major overruns, I estimate a cash outflow of ~$1.2 Bn over the next 4 years. These are broadly in line with the assumptions forecasted by APA management- i.e. ~250-300 million dollars per year of additional capex. This is likely to impact the FCF available to shareholders between 2025-2028, and will likely reduce the FCF yield.


Valuation

The FCF assumptions for APA are highly dependent on the global crude prices. To ensure we are taking into account a broad range of situations, I started with the unlevered cash flows for APA in the last 5 years. As you can see from the data below, 2022 (when avg. crude prices were ~$100/bbl) marks a potential ceiling for cashflows. On the other hand, 2023 & 2024 (when Avg. crude prices were ~$80/bbl) we see the avg cashflows ~$1bn. For 2025- I start with the TTM FCF as a base - when crude prices have been ~$80/bbl.

As per the management, the production is expected to slightly increase, but costs/bbl are expected to decline by ~15-18%. The cost reduction, coupled with ~15-19% lower capex may lead to an optimistic case scenario of ~30% increase in unlevered FCF. Even if we assume this relatively optimistic growth in FCF, I arrive at a fair market value of only ~$22.4/share which is almost equal to the current share price. This is partially driven by the high WACC expectation of the company, given it has a high beta of ~1.3 and the cash flows are extremely volatile.

Conclusion

Given I do not see a clear good news catalyst for the company in the near future, but multiple sources of potential bad news (e.g. increased oil supply by OPEC, removing of sanctions on Russia, slower industrial growth in China or over-run of Suriname costs) I would ideally rate the stock as a hold and wait for a better entry point.

data.title: New Listings Spring Early
data.description:
Despite snow banks and cold temps in much of Canada, property owners are getting a jump on what they hope will be strong spring demand.

New property listings leapt 11% nationwide from December to January, the largest monthly increase in almost two years.

The supply influx boosted the inventory of homes for sale in Canada to its highest level since the onset of the pandemic. But so far, it’s failed to attract the desired jump in home sales - the number of transactions dipped in the past two months, including a 3.3% decline in January.

Canada’s annual sales-to-new listings ratio is shown below since 2004. When this ratio dips below .40, market conditions favour buyers over sellers. A full-on buyer’s market has not been seen nationally since this ratio fell to .36 in November 2008. See RBC, Canadian Buyers unfazed by sellers’ early jump into the housing market.

Under the hood, the sales to new listings ratio varies significantly by metropolitan area (shown below). Toronto and Vancouver, at .33 and .37, respectively, were weaker in January than during the 2008-09 recession. In Edmonton, Calgary, and Montreal, supply/demand weightings still favour sellers.

In the first 18 days of February, the sales-to-new listing ratio in Toronto was .31, lower than in 2009 (shown below since 2006). Anyone looking to buy in areas with low sales-to-new listing ratios should not be afraid to make lowball offers and be patient as sellers become more realistic, aka ‘motivated.’

In the 2008 global recession, Canada fared better than most countries because it entered the downturn with relatively less inflated home prices and lower levels of household debt.

Unfortunately, what were tailwinds then have reversed into headwinds for Canada in 2025 (Canada’s household debt to GDP shown below since 2005).

The unnatural jump in Canada’s residential property prices from 2008-2022 is shown below (chart since 1970). Previous (lesser) overshoots have been corrected via sharp drops/years of flat prices. This time is unlikely to be different.

Canadians need a new national obsession and investment focus outside counter-productive housing speculation. It’s time to invest in innovation and productivity enhancements that lower costs and improve life quality. MCGA (make Canada great again)?

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Joby Aviation: Priced For Perfect Execution
data.description:
Since our last review of Joby Aviation (NYSE:JOBY), one of the leading companies at the forefront of eVTOL development, the stock has performed quite worse than the S&P 500 (SPY), with a return of only 5.19% versus 34.98% for the S&P 500. In that article, we showed our skepticism and gave Joby a hold rating because we thought the path to commercialization might be more unclear than investors expected.

About 1.5 years later, Joby has made tremendous progress in the interim, both in terms of achieving type certification for their aircraft and international developments in markets such as the UAE, South Korea and Japan that we discuss in this article. At a valuation of $6BN, however, we are concerned that Joby's price for Type Certification is almost a certainty and that much operational success is likely priced into our updated valuation model.

Working Towards Commercialization

In a way, Joby has recently begun to refute many of the naysayers who label eVTOL as a pipe dream, as much progress has been made toward FAA certification, and it looks more and more like commercialization can become a reality. Overall, Joby still needs to get type certification to fly commercially in the United States.

According to the latest earnings report, the company is just about done with the first 3 stages of certification, including the certification basis, the means of compliance and the submission of certification plans. The FAA is reportedly now through 21% of the Test & Analysis stage, while Joby is around 41% and has also made progress for stage 5. In terms of specific developments, Joby reported that it has tested a major aircraft structure for the first time with the FAA, completing static load testing for the tail structure.

In addition to completing static load testing for the tail structure, management also now claims that approximately 35% of composite parts made at Joby are built for FAA-compliant use in TIA aircraft and tested for credit, which is a remarkable achievement. In addition, Joby continues to make tremendous progress in international markets, with flights in both South Korea and Japan. In South Korea, for example, the company participated in the K-UAM Grand Challenge and became the first company to complete flights under the program.

It was in Japan not too long ago where Joby made its first international test flight outside the United States, at the Higashi-Fuji Technical Center in Shizuoka as a result of their long-standing partnership with Toyota (TM). This partnership with Toyota was seemingly strengthened in October, when the company announced an additional $500M investment in Joby in cash for common stock, with a $250M tranche well underway and another $250M tranche expected to close this year, depending on terms. This additional liquidity, along with $222M raised through a follow-on offering and the $710M in short-term cash, should bring Joby's total liquidity position to an estimated $1.4BN, virtually its strongest position since the IPO.

In another market, the Middle East, Joby has begun construction on one of 4 expected initial vertiports in the UAE. The first is the Dubai International Airport, where Joby plans to launch its services in late 2025 at the earliest. Although we think this may be optimistic and will probably take place sometime in 2026, given that Joby's timelines have proven to be rather optimistic in the past. Additionally, Joby is also working with Mukamalah, a subsidiary of the largest oil producer Saudi Aramco, to bring operations to Saudi Arabia, as they reportedly operate the world's largest fleet of corporate aircraft.

Perhaps the most significant regulatory development we have seen recently in the United States is the finalization of the new SFAR or Special Federal Aviation Regulation that was released for powered elevator and pilot certification. This is the first time in 80 years that a new category has been released for civil use since helicopters in the 1940s, which we believe indicates that the FAA is serious about marketing eVTOL. This final version of the SFAR has also addressed some concerns for Joby and other eVTOL makers when it comes to pilot training and energy reserves, which were considered too conservative in the original SFAR by leading developers in the space.

On pilot training, the SFAR includes a clear pathway for us to train pilots using the high-fidelity flight simulators we are qualifying alongside CAE and a single set of pilot controls. The rule also confirms our expectation that the energy reserves required in service will be equivalent to helicopter operations under VFR rules today. (Q3 Earnings Call)

Priced For Perfection

And yet, despite the significant progress being made, we are reluctant to have Joby as a Buy, which is mostly due to valuation concerns we have. JPMorgan also pointed this out recently, saying that the “hype may be too high” and that they think they are “acting as if type certification has already been successfully completed.”

Fundamentally, for a company worth $5.99BN, Joby is virtually still a company with almost zero revenue whose valuation depends entirely on theoretical future results. And it may be some time before the company trades at a reasonable valuation, given LTM operating income is -$575.12M with a free cash flow of -$432.18M for the same period. Overall, at a multiple of 18x, Joby should yield $314.44M annually, which is a $746.62M gap compared to where free cash flow currently stands.

The problem we see primarily is the fact that at a valuation of about $6BN, certification is more or less priced in, in our opinion, as is a certain degree of certainty that Joby will become a viable business model and become profitable at a fairly rapid pace. From a financing perspective, we think Joby is strongly positioned with $710M in cash and short-term investments, which combined with $222M additional raised through offerings and $500M from the Toyota investment should bring their balance sheet to $1.4BN.

Management expects FY2024 to have a cash outlay of $440M to $470M, which is likely to be closer to the lower end of the guided range, meaning that at the current burn rate Joby has just over 3 years of cash left. We think this should be enough to pass certification, although we still see the possibility of additional dilution to get through the early stages of scaling up production. In making our valuation, we also encountered the difficulty of not having details of what the ramp-up will look like over the long term and what unit profitability will ultimately look like, as they are mostly speculative. The only guidance we really have were estimates put out at the time of Joby's IPO through SPAC, which initially assumed a 2024 launch and cash flow profitability by the end of 2025, targets that in retrospect were far too optimistic.

Nonetheless, we decided to chart an optimistic scenario of what a successful ramp and certification could look like through 2034, as we see value being realized mainly in the very long term. In our optimistic estimates, we see Joby operating with over 2045 eVTOL by 2034 and generating $4.5BN in revenue, with each eVTOL generating $2.1M per year, as previously estimated by management.

As for cash burn, we see Joby becoming cash flow positive when the threshold of 400 operational eVTOL is crossed, which in an optimistic scenario would be in 2028. While the ramp-up may seem steep in the first few years, we believe Joby can meet these goals with Toyota as an operating partner in manufacturing.

Although management believes in their SPAC presentation that they can generate more than 40% adjusted EBITDA margins, we think this is probably too optimistic, like most other forecasts made a few years ago, and we have calculated that free cash flow margins will grow to 20% by 2034, which should still be above the average in the airline/ride-sharing sector. As for the discount factor in the DCF model, we came up with 12.58%, which is mainly based on a cost of equity of 12.68%, since Joby has very little long-term debt. This cost of equity includes a beta of 2.33, a risk-free interest rate of 4.48% based on the 10-year Treasury, and an expected return of 8%.

Applying a reasonable growth rate of 3%, we are left with $9.67BN, which after discounting amounts to $2.96BN. Adjusting for incoming discounted cash flows during the period and net debt, we are left with $4.56BN of equity value, or $5.96 per share. This is well below the $7.83 per share the company is currently trading at.

Moreover, there are many risks associated with this optimistic statement, even if Joby can achieve type certification somewhere later this year or in the near future. For example, the eVTOL market itself is expected to grow to $4.67BN by 2030, according to market research, at which point we estimate Joby's revenues to be $2.1BN, meaning they would represent nearly 45% of the entire market.

Perhaps the biggest risks lie in the fact that the demand outlook is uncertain, as these eVTOLs have yet to reach the commercial market on a large scale. Especially with Joby, given that they are going for the direct route to consumers by offering an air taxi service themselves, compared to its closest competitor Archer Aviation (ACHR) which plans to sell eVTOL directly and already has an order book of more than $6 billion. Even compared to the traditional helicopter market, Joby would be one of the largest aircraft manufacturers in 10 years with more than 2,000 aircraft, as just over 9,300 commercial helicopters were in operation in the United States in 2019 alone.

And while the prospects for eVTOL could be significant in terms of competition for traditional ride-sharing on the ground with cost improvements in fuel and an improved level of autonomy, there are operators such as Blade (BLDE) that offer similar services for low-cost short-haul flights, but generate only $241.81M on a TTM basis, are on track to have sold more than 155K seats in 2024 and still have negative operating income.

The Bottom Line

Although Joby is getting closer to type certification of its aircraft and has made significant progress in terms of their liquidity, estimated at $1.4 billion, we believe the company needs near-perfect execution to live up to its current valuation.

We believe that not only will Joby likely be priced for successful type certification, but also that the company will be able to ramp up to over 2,000 operating eVTOL over the next 10 years while generating an impressive 20% free cash flow margin over the long term. Even if it were able to live up to these estimates, we see the fair value being more likely to be around $5.96 per share, which is a downward drop of 24% from where the stock is currently trading. As things stand, with the current valuation, we believe there are too many operational and execution risks to warrant a buy rating.

Seeking Alpha's Quant currently has Joby as “Buy,” with the positive factors being profitability and momentum, while valuation, growth and earnings revisions remain a drag on the rating.

data.title: Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals: Progress During Adverse Market Conditions
data.description:
Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:ARWR) reported fiscal Q1 2025 results and provided a pipeline update last week. The good news was that zodasiran is back in the clinic for the treatment of a rare disease called HoFH, and that we may see data updates from up to three programs partnered in late 2024 with Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) – ARO-DM1, ARO-DUX4, and ARO-MMP7.

The bad news, well, there was no bad news from the company, but the problem in the near-term is that Arrowhead is still lumped into a group of genetic stocks that are being relentlessly sold for an extended period of time, and the stock is down approximately 15% since my last public article in May 2024. As such, I am not terribly optimistic about sentiment changing for the stock in the near-term if it does not change for the group.

But it is also not impossible to break away from the group, and a powerful catalyst (or two) could change the sentiment. One standout performer in the group is BioNTech (BNTX), which performed well in the last 12 months due to company-specific updates from its oncology pipeline – it has one of the more advanced clinical candidates in a hot area – a PD-L1/VEGF antibody.

In today’s article, I will provide updated thoughts following the financial results and the pipeline update.

Zodasiran is back in the clinic

Of all the programs that were announced as non-core last year, I was most disappointed to see zodasiran's de-prioritization. The company is still reluctant to do cardiovascular outcomes trials of zodasiran and/or plozasiran, but I believe that a small phase 3 trial in homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (‘HoFH’) is a no-brainer. The cost of the trial will be modest, the indication is de-risked with a shared target with Regeneron’s (REGN) Evkeeza, and Evkeeza is showing a path to zodasiran adding $200 million+ to Arrowhead’s topline at peak and more than $100 million to the bottom line due to the significant operating leverage through the use of the plozasiran sales force.

Evkeeza generated $125.7 million in net sales in 2024 with 60% Y/Y growth in each of the four quarters of 2024. And zodasiran is well-positioned to compete here with what should be similar efficacy, and convenient quarterly subcutaneous injections versus once-monthly IV infusions of Evkeeza.

Potential updates from three Sarepta-partnered programs

Arrowhead continues to enroll the phase 1/2 trials of ARO-DM1, ARO-DUX4, and ARO-MMP7, and since late 2025, these programs are partnered with Sarepta Therapeutics.

Arrowhead's management said on last week's earnings call that we may see data from all three trials later this year, but that disclosures are subject to agreement with Sarepta. If these trials are generating good data, I am sure Sarepta will waste little time and that it will allow Arrowhead to provide data updates.

Sarepta willing to pay $300 million in milestone payments for enrollment targets in the ARO-DM1 trial is unusual and the trial is enrolling up to 48 patients. This “smells” like registrational intent on Sarepta’s side, but we still need to see positive data from the trial for that to happen.

The other two candidates could also rapidly progress to registrational trials, and while I am not optimistic on ARO-MMP7 for the idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, it is interesting that Sarepta wanted this program, so, there might be an efficacy signal in this early trial as well.

Plozasiran will be ready for launch in late 2025, SHTG trials to complete enrollment in mid-2025

The Breakthrough Therapy Designation was of no help to plozasiran – the FDA gave a 12-month review period for plozasiran for the treatment of familial chylomicronemia syndrome (‘FCS’). The approval of Ionis Pharmaceuticals’ (IONS) Tryngolza (olezarsen) is to blame here since it addresses the unmet need for this population, and makes the FDA less interested in a fast review of a second product for the same condition.

Plozasiran has either met or exceeded the expectations I laid out in May 2024 article. It delivered significant triglyceride reductions in the phase 3 trial in FCS patients and it demonstrated a statistically significant reduction in acute pancreatitis events.

While we wait for the PDUFA date for plozasiran, we should get three quarterly sales updates from Ionis which will show how the demand looks like in this very small market.

However, I want to note that this indication’s importance will be short-lived for both companies.

Ionis will complete the phase 3 trials of olezarsen in severe hypertriglyceridemia patients (‘SHTG’) in late 2025 and it will likely launch it in the first half of 2027, and Arrowhead is slightly behind as it will complete enrollment of plozasiran in SHTG patients in mid-2025 and it will likely receive approval and launch plozasiran for the treatment of this population in the second half of 2027. This means that the price of plozasiran and olezarsen will fall by a factor of, probably 25 or 30, and whatever FCS sales there are will be divided by that number – if the annualized net sales run rate is $100 million, it will go down to $3-4 million and if it is $300 million, it will go down to $10-12 million.

I will reiterate again that the long-term value of the FCS indication is very low, and that the SHTG indication is far more important and should unlock a significantly larger market than the FCS market will ever be even at the current price of Tryngolza of more than half a million dollars per patient per year.

ARO-INHBE and ARO-ALK7 to produce proof-of-concept data in obese patients 2H 2025 and 1H 2026

The ARO-INHBE trial started dosing obese volunteers in late 2024 and Arrowhead expects to have preliminary data from the single-ascending dose part of the trial in the second half of the year. And single doses should be more than sufficient to provide proof-of-concept – the weight loss effect and the impact on body composition, thanks to the long durability of effect of the drug.

ARO-ALK7 will start dosing obese volunteers in mid-2025 and this suggests we will see the preliminary data in the first half of 2026. As a reminder, this is Arrowhead’s first adipose candidate, and positive proof-of-concept data could lead to expansion of this side of the platform.

New core programs are coming

The cardiometabolic pipeline will get a new candidate this year – the CTA filing for the first dimer (a candidate reducing the expression of two target genes) is expected later this year and the targets are APOC3 and PSCK9. This should be a very powerful cardiovascular drug, basically a combination of plozasiran and Novartis' (NVS) and Alnylam's (ALNY) Leqvio (inclisiran), as it should significantly reduce triglycerides, remnant cholesterol, and LDL cholesterol.

The CNS pipeline is progressing as well, and while some of the candidates were given to Sarepta, there are other candidates that Arrowhead still wholly owns. CTAs for ARO-HTT for the treatment of Huntington’s disease (this one is partnered with Sarepta) and ARO-MAPT for the treatment of Alzheimer’s disease are expected toward the end of the year and the CTA for ARO-SNCA for the treatment of Parkinson’s disease is expected in 2026.

The company has still not determined whether CNS will be one of the core platforms and the decisions will be data-driven. Management’s current thinking is to keep ARO-MAPT in-house and to try and find a partner for ARO-SNCA.

Other non-core programs are waiting for partners

The rest of the pipeline is now non-core and will not progress without partners – ARO-RAGE, ARO-C3, ARO-CFB, ARO-PNPLA3. I am not really optimistic about the partnering possibilities for these candidates, but would like to see ARO-CFB and ARO-C3 developed for complement-mediated diseases.

Financial overview – Arrowhead is in the best financial shape ever

Arrowhead ended calendar 2024 with $553 million in cash and equivalents. The deal with Sarepta closed last week and it will add $825 million to the company’s balance sheet for a pro-forma cash position of $1.38 billion. Arrowhead is also entitled to receive $50 million a year from Sarepta for the next five years, and to another $300 million in near-term milestone payments that are tied to enrollment targets in the phase 1/2 trial of ARO-DM1 which the company expects to hit in the following quarters.

In the next two years, Arrowhead expects the operating cash burn to be in the $500-550 million range, and that the cash balance will be sufficient to fund the company into 2028. The graph below shows how the cash position evolution should look like in the next five quarters, and this includes $350 million in additional payments from Sarepta in calendar Q4 2025 and Q1 2026.

Arrowhead’s cash position can further improve in the following quarters and years from additional milestone payments from Sarepta, other existing partners, new partners, plozasiran revenues or royalty-backed deals.

The times when we had to think about where the next cash injection will come from are behind us, but management is still reluctant to run some studies or clinical programs – primarily the cardiovascular outcomes trial of plozasiran.

Conclusion

I am not very optimistic about the near-term share price performance due to the negative biotech sentiment and even worse sentiment in Arrowhead’s area of genetic stocks, but the fundamentals continue to improve and the cash injection from Sarepta and upcoming milestone payments allow the company to focus on pipeline execution and partnering discussions with far less pressure and urgency.

I am looking forward to the updates from several programs in the following quarters, most of all from ARO-INHBE and ARO-DM1 trials this year, and from ARO-ALK7's trial next year.

data.title: RSPF: This Is Why Cap Weighted ETFs Are So Popular
data.description:
ETFs can be very complex now that options-infused strategies, leverage and inverse products have exploded onto the scene in record numbers. I'm a big fan of all of the above, just not across the board, since there are many ways to have that "explosion" occur in one's portfolio if they don't know how to handle the flexibility they've been handed. This is particularly a risk that self-directed investors need to be aware of.

Comparatively, another key feature of some ETFs is a lot easier to understand. I'm referring to capitalization weighting, the "normal" way in that the most popular equity ETFs are structured that way.

But for years, industry watchers have been telling us that equal-weighted indexing is going to have its day. That's where each stock's portion of the portfolio is the same, and not based on the size of the stock. The advantages are essentially in the math. An equal weighted ETF, even if it owns the same stocks as a cap weighted fund, can smooth the ride for investors, without concern that a few "big guys" in the ETF weighting have a colossal earnings miss or some other disruption.

The opposite is true as well in that those same big stocks can get bigger and use their expanded size to crush competition. It can feed on itself that way for years and produce a market climate where the "favored few" in a sector can take up all the air in the room, and the room in the portfolio. In fact, that's what has been happening for years, in nearly every sector. Of the 11 S&P 500 sectors, all but one (Industrials, symbol XLI) are top heavy.

And that brings me to the financial sector, which is often the biggest sector in "value" portfolios. There might be a time when the equal-weighted version of the financial stocks within the S&P 500, Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Financial (NYSEARCA:RSPF) rivals its cap weighted peer, SPDRs Select Sector Financial ETF (XLF).

I would like to see funds like RSPF be more in demand from investors, rather than so much money going into funds like XLF. But I don't see that happening too quickly. Because even if the big financial stocks did a "2008-style" face plant, the way markets now work, the rest of the financials will likely fall for two reasons. One is sentiment-driven, the other is structural.

The sentiment part is the old Wall Street concept of stocks "falling in sympathy." For instance, if JPMorgan (JPM) were to suddenly become the market's public enemy number one, that would cast such a pall over the broader sector, I doubt investors would flock to other banks in a way that would stick beyond a few weeks or months.

The structural aspect is all about the so-called "ETF-ization" of the markets. This is where so much passive money is now present, stock-picking is that much tougher than 10-15 years ago. That's one big reason I like ETFs. But it's also why I caution investors to either be selective about their single stock efforts or at least employ some sort of risk management. This is what I call the "how" of investing. I know what I want to buy and when I want to buy it. But do I do so in big size, smaller size, with options around it, with options instead of the stock, etc.?

RSPF is a classic modern example of how ETFs have been great, but how they might also be dangerous for investors misjudging how they work and what's unique about each one. Case in point, here's RSPF vs. XLF, one-year rolling returns since 2007. They rise and fall together, nearly all of the time.

Logically, they should have gathered assets around the same pace. Nope. RSPF did grow assets by 1,000 since 2008, but XLF, which was much bigger to start (it was first listed in the late 1990s), has grown by more than 5,000% over that time. The tally now: XLF, $54 billion to RSPF's $320 million. Yikes, that a big difference.

RSPF will be a distant second choice unless/until investors look at stock investing differently

Is it justified? I think so. Sure the marketing of the "sector SPDRs" has been intense for years. But I think investors want it as simple as possible, and if cap weighting isn't broken, they have no need to fix it.

There's also the investment purest in me that looks at ETFs as simply baskets of stocks I might otherwise own directly, but prefer to have some cushion around them. XLF provides that cushion, RSPF does too. But the latter has way too much cushion. At least, not if it won't consistently outperform. There's no alpha there.

Here they are, side by side. Same 76 stocks, but based on stock size, only about 10-20 of them matter. That's what XLF's comparable returns to RSPF tell me. So, why bother owning all of those other stocks, that as an ETF researcher, I will want to at least keep an eye on? I'm not a big fan of investing in financials since I firmly believe there's some unfinished business from the financial crisis of 2007-2009. But as noted earlier, in this sector, I don't see equal weighting as the solution. Not having as much exposure to financial stocks is a better solution, should the clouds darken again.

Holdings analysis

The top 10 in an equal weight ETF tend to reflect the recent winners since they're all set to the same weight at each rebalance date. So that's what we see here. Some of the major stocks in the sector are here, and others have slightly lower weightings.

We can get some idea of the factor makeup of the sector here. Lots of overvalued names, but clearly on a roll, given those momentum grades. This sector is far from cheap, but it has kept the strong pace going, frankly for longer than I ever would have expected. I've missed out through underweighting here, but I'm OK with it, since I'm not a sector wonk. I just want to make money and not lose big along the way.

ETFs as a stock research tool

Some investors quickly dismiss ETFs, but to me they're not just investments, they're great research tools. Simply by scanning this top 25 holdings list, I can see several low PE stocks that might be worth some additional work at the single stock level. I strongly encourage investors to look at ETFs in that way too, if they're primarily stock pickers.

The other standout column here's the one-year returns, which have been strong across the board. That's where RSPF's valuation grade comes from. We're looking at one-third of the stocks the ETF here.

I will note that the five-year earnings growth, at around 70% of the trailing PE ratio shown below, is on the attractive side, relatively speaking.

There's not much yield here, or anywhere in the US equity market these days. Not without a lot of risk. And I'm all about managing risk!

RSPF: Not bad, but not worth the investment for me

My research into this article was driven in part by my desire to see if a sector I simply don't care much for, financials, might offer a window of opportunity vs. XLF. It doesn't. RSPF is equal to the task, but there's not enough here for me to want to own it in my portfolio. That said, it isn't broken at this point, so I rate it "hold" since sell and buy would not make sense to me here.

data.title: XFLT And EIC: Double-Digit Yields To Boost Your Income
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Today, we are looking at two collateralized loan obligation-focused closed-end funds to boost your income significantly. These are higher yielders, paying into the double-digits. Of course, higher yielders can often come with higher risks. These certainly aren't risk-free investments, either. These are closed-end funds that are leveraged, meaning they are utilizing borrowings to help bump up their yields.

In addition to the added volatility that leverage brings, CEFs are subject to wildly varying discounts/premiums. That is the market price that investors can buy and sell shares at can vary from the actual underlying net asset value per share. That increases volatility further, but it can also provide opportunities where that can be exploited to benefit investors.

XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT)

  • 1-Year Z-score: -2.04.
  • Premium/Discount: -0.76%.
  • Distribution Yield: 14.19%.
  • Expense Ratio: 3.91% (8.33% when including leverage expenses).
  • Leverage: 38.58%.
  • Managed Assets: $816 million.
  • Structure: Perpetual.

XFLT's objective is to "seek attractive total return with an emphasis on income generation across multiple stages of the credit cycle."

They will do this through "a dynamically managed portfolio of floating-rate credit instruments and other structured credit investments within the private markets. Under normal market conditions, the Trust will invest at least 80% of managed assets in senior secured loans, CLO debt and equity."

XFLT takes a hybrid approach and isn't only invited in CLOs. In fact, the largest exposure of the fund is listed as senior secured first lien investments at nearly 46% of the portfolio.

The last time we were talking about XFLT, we were discussing the distribution cut. As we noted, the fund will be susceptible to interest rate changes. Just as the fund benefitted from the Fed bumping rates up aggressively, driving up its income generation. Well, the opposite is also true. The Fed cut 100 basis points and that put pressure on the fund's net investment income that it could generate as yields started to come down.

This year, we could see another 50 basis points of cuts, though that really depends on the data. It originally looked like 50 basis points, but as inflation remains sticky, potential trade war escalations with tariffs could also keep it around for longer. Therefore, it wouldn't be too surprising to see the Fed hold rates steady throughout the year. Either way, I believe that XFLT cut its distribution sufficiently enough to expect that it will remain at the current level for at least the next 12 months—that's if the Fed cuts another 50 basis points or not.

This 9.4% distribution cut was uncomfortable for some investors. Since then, we've seen the fund's premium shrink. After many years of trading at a premium regularly, the fund has even begun to start flirting with trading at a discount level. I believe that opens up a longer-term opportunity for investors to be able to scoop up and add to their position. I know that I, personally, added, so I didn't end up seeing a cut in the distribution as my income grew with the added shares.

On the other hand, there is a bit of a downside when a fund like XFLT starts trading at or near a discount. That is an investor not being able to take advantage of the discounted dividend reinvestment plan. For XFLT, if the fund is trading at a premium, investors can DRIP and receive shares at up to a 5% discount or NAV, whichever is higher.

Eagle Point Income Co Inc (EIC)

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.82.
  • Premium/Discount: 7.98% (based on 12/31/24 estimated NAV of $14.94-$15.04).
  • Distribution Yield: 15.03%.
  • Expense Ratio: 3.54% (6.60% when including leverage expenses).
  • Leverage: 31.95%.
  • Managed Assets: $458.2 million.
  • Structure: Perpetual.

EIC's investment objective is "to generate high current income, with a secondary objective to generate capital appreciation." To achieve this, they will "invest primarily in junior debt tranches of CLOs. In addition, we intend to invest up to 35% of our total assets in CLO equity securities and related securities and instruments."

EIC is a more CLO-focused fund, where nearly its entire portfolio is invited in CLOs. However, unlike its older and larger brother, Eagle Point Credit Co LLC (ECC), the fund has a higher exposure to the debt tranches of CLOs. More specifically, the BB-rated CLO debt tranches.

All else being equal, that can make EIC relatively less risky compared to ECC. The equity tranche is there to take losses before the debt slices. As one would naturally expect to be the case, we can see that over time, the cumulative default rates for global CLOs in the upper tranches have been very minimal.

When you get to the BB level, there are naturally some defaults and the longer the time horizon, the more likely that is to occur, as expected. That said, defaults have historically occurred at a very low rate, even when you get out to the 10-year time horizon.

EIC launched just prior to the Covid pandemic. Since then, we can take a look at the comparison of EIC to ECC. Interestingly, on a total NAV return basis, we see that EIC has delivered a materially worse relative performance. On the other hand, we have the total share price returns that have favored EIC over ECC.

It is worth noting that the funds provide only monthly estimated NAVs, so that can make the total NAV return chart look a bit more unusual. Either way, we know the cause of this occurring, which will be the discount/premium mechanic at play in the CEF structure. ECC enjoys trading at a healthy premium, but it was incredibly elevated toward the end of 2019 and heading into 2020. As these premiums diverged to start trading similarly more recently, that's what saw the total share price returns from EIC deliver a more attractive result.

Similar to XFLT, EIC (and ECC) also enjoy a discounted DRIP where investors can take advantage of reinvested at discounted market prices. This can be worthwhile even for an investor who needs the cash to survive because the shares can easily be liquidated for the cash needed. That can still allow an income-focused investor who needs that income the cash needed for their bills.

EIC cut its distribution during Covid, but after that, had increased it many times.

With the Fed implementing 100 basis points of cuts, I suspect the raises are probably over at this point. They could even choose to reduce the distribution similar to what XFLT had done. They last reported NII and realized gains per share of $0.57, and comparing that to paying $0.60, we saw there was a shortfall.

That NII plus realized gains were for the three months that ended September 30, 2024, meaning that the Fed rate cuts weren't having an impact at that point. Further, when factoring in NII alone, we can subtract out the $0.08 in realized gains and see an NII of $0.49. That would pressure the coverage even further.

With their next earnings coming up later this month, we should get a better idea of the impact on NII. However, it really won't be even better known until the Q1 2025 results. If they do cut the distribution by an aggressive 20% to line up the distribution with the last quarterly NII, we would still be looking at a 12%+ distribution rate.

With that being said, they may choose not to cut the payout at all. In fact, they just announced the next round and maintained the same $0.20. Further, if they can continue to issue new shares accretively and those realized gains can be reinvested successfully elsewhere, that could limit the NII pressure.

Conclusion

Today, we took a look at XFLT and EIC, two funds that invest in the CLO space. XFLT is more of a hybrid, incorporating a meaningful sleeve of individual senior loan positions. These aren't risk-free investments by any means, but they are both attractive options for boosting one's income massively, thanks to their double-digit yields.

data.title: First Solar Stock: Deep Analysis Reveals Solar's Geopolitical Importance
data.description:
First Solar, Inc. (NASDAQ:FSLR) stock is in a persistent downtrend with a -22% loss since my last analysis amid fears that the Trump administration will withdraw the company's federal tax credit benefits in the near future. However, such an action without alternative strong support for solar power would be a short-term gain for the United States Treasury, but a long-term loss for the country's energy independence and security. Utility-scale solar power infrastructure remains the most critical long-term energy asset, and China currently retains a wide market lead in this regard. Catching up to China here may be impossible, but it is important that the United States prepares well in advance to protect from dependency dynamics evolving over many decades.

Due to the foresight of the current Trump administration and surrounding advisors, including the Department of Government Efficiency, I expect First Solar and its proponents will continue to be fortified politically. As such, a sentiment reversal seems likely for First Solar stock in the near-term future when Trump and his administration have more publicly outlined their stance on renewable energy infrastructure development in the United States moving forward.

Tax Credit Analysis

The new Trump administration's most negative impact on the solar industry so far has been on short-term investor sentiment. If the Trump administration intends to repeal the Inflation Reduction Act (“IRA”), which provides substantial tax incentives for clean energy, First Solar investors have a reason to be concerned in the short term. Even in the immediate aftermath of Trump being elected, First Solar stock dropped around 12% in price, primarily reflecting this possible elimination of federal tax credits.

Trump is not a particularly good prospect for First Solar at face value, considering that in 2024 the company anticipated $1 billion to $1.05 billion in cash proceeds from the Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit under Section 45X of the Internal Revenue Code; results are expected to be reported on February 25th.

However, this elimination of federal tax credits for First Solar is currently speculative. New executive orders from the President do not impact current energy tax incentives.

Even in a worst-case scenario where the federal tax credits are annihilated with guidance from the Department of Government Efficiency (an action I would deem wise to avoid if possible), First Solar would remain in a robust domestic position if Trump's tariffs heavily penalize anti-dumping practices from China and other international manufacturing centers of excellence.

However, if the Trump administration repeals federal tax credits and subsequently fails to put into place stringent anti-dumping measures (for example, to prevent JinkoSolar (JKS) from China undercutting First Solar in the domestic U.S. market) on top of moderate trade tariffs, the United States solar energy position (an extremely valuable long-term geopolitical play) could be severely weakened.

First Solar remains the United States' most dominant solar panel producer, operating with a full-stack, vertically integrated and domestic manufacturing model. The Trump administration and future U.S. governments would be wise to safeguard it, and given the company's impact on America's domestic manufacturing strength, I believe Trump has the foresight to work to preserve this. This creates a bullish framework for First Solar investors to consider, despite present bearishness indicated in the enduring stock price downtrend.

First Solar's domestic nameplate annual manufacturing capacity is set to increase to 14 GW by 2026. First Solar is the West's largest fully vertically integrated solar manufacturer. The company does not manufacture in China. — Rodzianko's own words, sourced from Business Wire, First Solar.

Energy Analysis

In 2023, the U.S. consumed nearly 19 million barrels per day of oil, but the Energy Information Administration forecasts that U.S. oil demand will rise to 20.5 million barrels per day in 2025. Under President Trump's second term, U.S. oil production is projected to reach record levels; U.S. crude oil output will likely increase to 13.6 million barrels per day in 2026. However, the Energy Information Administration forecasts that the global supply of crude oil and other liquid hydrocarbons will only be adequate to meet world demand through 2050.

Trump, renowned as a tactician, is utilizing America's crude oil production capacity for short-term strategic gain, but the long-term outlook ought not to be forgotten. Without a robust clean energy infrastructure, the United States will likely lose the long-term energy competition against China, the world's foremost producer of solar panels.

Renewable energy sources are forecasted to become 65% to 80% of global power generation by 2050, while fossil fuels' contribution to the global supply is expected to decrease from 80% today to around 20% by 2050. However, the IEA's 20% 2050 target is based on stringent net-zero goals, which appear idealistic and unreasonable.

As of data from 2023, China generates around 44% of its electricity from renewable sources, a vast lead to just 21% in the United States. Further delays in domestic renewable energy adoption in America may increase its reliance on China for critical future energy components like solar panels and batteries. Such reliance may result in significant economic and national security risks. Therefore, First Solar and other U.S. solar panel manufacturers are unlikely to be neglected by the current U.S. Government and following administrations.

A failure to commit to renewable energy infrastructure now may also destabilize the United States' global economic leadership position in the long term. When solar power (most certainly) becomes the predominant form of power provision on the planet, China could be deemed the dominant energy market leader, with an impenetrable moat developed patiently and devoutly over decades. As such, First Solar remains critical to America's long-term geopolitical interests, an assessment the Trump administration will likely also realize. This is fundamental to the long-term bull case for First Solar, but I consider it prudent to monitor the stock and wait for a sentiment reversal before heavily allocating capital.

Valuation Analysis

The following price targets show the theoretical impacts of the IRA being repealed (bear case), the IRA modified to moderately lower tax credits (base case), and being sustained as currently instated (bull case). My targets also consider weak solar panel anti-dumping policies from the Trump administration (bear case), moderately strong anti-dumping policies (base case), and strong anti-dumping policies (bull case). The current stock price is $163.

Sentiment Analysis

First Solar stock is in bearish territory, made evident by the 50-day moving average crossing down past the 200-day moving average, and the Relative Strength Index value being around 40. I do not expect this bear market to change in the immediate future. The new political dynamics under Trump deserve caution, even though they primarily affect investor sentiment for solar companies more than their financials for now.

The primary risk that investors face is that the repeal of IRA tax credits will cause further selling action. At this point, significantly lower valuations may occur in the short term. These lower valuations would potentially open up an opportunity to buy First Solar stock as a medium- to long-term holding, but now remains a highly precarious entry point. The reduction in short-term profitability from the repeal of IRA tax credits wouldn't destroy First Solar's competitive product advantages and long-term growth potential, but it will delay this growth significantly. Though the current valuation is appealing, for the highest alpha, it's best to have some patience and wait for a sentiment reversal and a clearer stance on clean energy policies from the Trump administration as they impact First Solar.

Q4 Preview

For Q4 2024, due on February 25th, the company is likely to have delivered robust earnings growth of around 50% year-over-year. Revenue is also likely to be up nearly 30% year-over-year. These factors are due to strong current market demand, record production, and competent cost management. This could very well act as a moment of sentiment stabilization, as continued earnings growth and margin expansion is probable throughout FY25. The company's new Alabama facility, capable of 3.5 GW of annual production, came online in September 2024. Its Louisiana facility, capable of the same annual production capacity, is anticipated to come online in H2 2025. Moreover, tariffs instigated by Trump on China will affect solar panels, supporting domestic demand for First Solar's products. First Solar has around 95% of its revenue from the United States.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

My base-case price target indicates a two-year annual return of around 50%, making the investment a Strong Buy as it stands. However, for capital allocators like myself who want the ideal moment to invest, negative returns will likely continue to persist for a while longer, making it prudent to wait. We need a stronger stance from the Trump administration on whether, and how, it plans to support First Solar and whether the IRA will continue to be a part of this for the indefinite future.

With a reasonable modification and not a complete repeal of tax credits, First Solar will still have a very strong near-term return horizon. Demand for solar panels remains robust, and America needs to secure its long-term energy independence with renewable energy just as much as it requires short-term tactical geopolitical security through fossil fuels.

data.title: Entrada Therapeutics: Cash-Rich Innovator With Asymmetric Upside
data.description:
Biotech investors sometimes overlook companies solving fundamental drug development problems. Entrada Therapeutics (NASDAQ:TRDA) operates in one such space, solving the enduring problem of intracellular drug delivery. Although much of the sector is under capital constraints and shifting regulatory pressures, Entrada's solid position does not seem to be appreciated by the market yet. A specialized approach and a strategy that combines internal development with strategic partnerships position the company for a risk-reward profile that the market has yet to price in.

Financials

Entrada Therapeutics has a financial position that affords it a level of stability that few small-cap biotech firms can claim - especially pre-commercial ones. It had $449.3 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities as of Q3 2024, up from $352 million as of 2023. Those gains were driven mainly by a $100 million direct offering and a $75 million milestone payment related to its partnership with Vertex Pharmaceuticals. While many early-stage biotech firms can barely extend their runway without excessive dilution, Entrada has bolstered its balance sheet through the aforementioned direct offering and strategic collaborations such as the Vertex partnership - a strategy that has minimized immediate reliance on additional fundraising.

Entrada has strong cash reserves but reported a $14 million net loss for Q3 2024. Increased investment in the lead Duchenne muscular dystrophy candidate ENTR-601-44 and pipeline expansion have pushed research and development expenses to $31.3 million compared with the prior year. High personnel costs also drove higher general and administrative costs, suggesting the company is preparing for increased clinical activity. The company's debt is also manageable, standing at $61 million - this is reasonable given its pre-commercial state, and the burn is being managed carefully. However, the only reason the Q3 2024 and Q1 2024 had net profits was due to one-time payments I mentioned earlier. Aside from this, the company's net loss has been trending down. Based on this trend, I would conservatively estimate Entrada's cash burn rate looking forward at $40-50 million per quarter, so its cash runway is likely to stretch into 2027. This longer time frame reduces near-term financing risk and allows the company to focus on execution rather than capital markets volatility. Of course, this is a conservative estimate - if R&D costs plateau, it is possible the burn will be much less than that.

Perhaps most interesting is Entrada's sharp drop in collaboration revenue, from $43.7 million in Q3 2023 to $19.6 million in Q3 2024. It owes its drop to lower expenses associated with its Vertex-partnered VX-670 program, which suggests that while past milestones have been lucrative, future revenue contributions from partnerships could be sporadic. This puts more pressure on the internal pipeline progress - ENTR-601-44 - to build investor confidence.

A potential concern with Entrada is its relatively low liquidity. The dangers of dilution are always a reasonable apprehension that investors have, but as I stated earlier, I believe that the company has sufficient cash reserves to fund development without relying on immediate capital infusion. Due to this, I think the risk in the near to mid-term stemming from liquidity or related issues is low.

Drug Pipeline

Entrada Therapeutics' pipeline is built around its Endosomal Escape Vehicle platform for improved intracellular delivery of oligonucleotide-based therapies. This platform is the core mechanism for ENTR-601-44, the lead candidate for Duchenne muscular dystrophy in patients with exon-44 mutations. The company recently received clearance from the UK MHRA to commence ELEVATE-44-201, a Phase 1/2 multiple ascending dose trial - its first major clinical step in this space. While exon-skipping therapies have had mixed results due to delivery challenges and variable dystrophin restoration, Entrada's strategy seeks to overcome these limitations through increased cellular uptake. If its tech supports meaningful dystrophin production while maintaining an excellent safety profile, the drug could carve out a niche where options are slim.

Beyond DMD, Entrada's work on VX-670 with Vertex Pharmaceuticals is another interesting avenue. It targets RNA mis-splicing disorders, specifically overcoming challenges of injecting oligonucleotides into muscle tissue. The involvement of Vertex suggests confidence in Entrada's delivery mechanism, a bottleneck in many genetic therapies. But given declining collaboration revenue in recent quarters, it's unclear how aggressively Vertex plans to push VX-670 forward. If the program enters later-stage trials, it may provide non-dilutive capital in the form of future milestone payments that would help Entrada grow its cash runway even more.

The wider pipeline includes additional oligonucleotide-based programs ENTR-601-45 and ENTR-601-50, likely targeting other DMD subpopulations. These early-stage assets also signal that Entrada is thinking beyond the single-drug model that could provide long-term value if its EEV platform proves broadly applicable. The key risk is whether the technology can translate into clinical success at a rate that distinguishes it from existing exon-skipping approaches. With first patient trials expected to begin in 2025, investors should be getting an early readout on how Entrada's science holds up in human trials.

EEV and Splicing Mechanisms

Drug delivery may be the most important driver of treatment success for Entrada. Some genetic diseases like Duchenne muscular dystrophy and RNA mis-splicing disorders require precise intracellular delivery of therapeutic molecules. Traditional approaches based on antisense oligonucleotides or phosphorodiamidate morpholino oligomers have shown poor uptake into muscle cells and weak clinical efficacy. The company's endosomal escape vehicle technology is designed to address this issue by improving endosomal release - one of the biggest barriers to oligonucleotide therapeutics. If delivered systemically, such molecules are usually trapped in endosomes and degraded before reaching their target inside the cell. Entrada's EEVs contain an endosomal escape-enhancing cyclic peptide motif that allows more active drugs to reach the nucleus and engage their genetic targets. This is important in muscle tissue where drug penetration has traditionally been a problem.

Mutations in DMD prevent the production of dystrophin, a structural protein needed for muscle integrity in Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Without it, muscle fibers become weak, lose function and degenerate. Exon-skipping therapies like Entrada's ENTR-601-44 let cells "skip over" the mutated exon and resume production of a shorter but functional dystrophin protein. Unlike typical exon-skipping drugs that show poor intracellular uptake, Entrada's EEV-PMO conjugate enhances delivery into muscle cells and increases dystrophin restoration. It is possible that this may be a key differentiator, as exon-skipping therapies have been described as having poor clinical efficacy despite measurable dystrophin uptake.

The Vertex-partnered VX-670 program targets RNA mis-splicing-related diseases, specifically CUG repeat expansion disorders such as myotonic dystrophy type 1. In DM1, toxic RNA traps MBNL1, a key splicing regulator, causing widespread splicing defects in several genes. These toxic RNA repeats bind to the specifically designed VX-670 to release MBNL1 and resume normal splicing. Muscle penetration for oligonucleotides in DM1 has always been a problem. The EEV platform may offer an advantage in getting higher drug concentrations into the skeletal and cardiac muscle where the disease is most damaging. If it is successful, it would open up doors to similar RNA-targeting approaches in other neuromuscular diseases.

Regulatory Green Light Marks a Crucial Inflection Point

Entrada Therapeutics received clearance from the UK Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency to initiate the ELEVATE-44-201 clinical trial of its lead candidate, ENTR-601-44. This is its first major step into patient trials and a critical test of its Endosomal Escape Vehicle technology. Although encouraging preclinical and early human safety data have shown uptake and exon skipping, real-world efficacy in Duchenne muscular dystrophy patients will be the pivotal point. The study will assess safety, pharmacokinetics, and dystrophin restoration in patients with exon 44 mutations, starting in Q2 2025.

The study has a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design that allows an objective assessment of ENTR-601-44's clinical impact. Unlike other early-stage biotech trials that rely on open-label data (generally more subject to patient bias, since researchers and patients know the treatments being given), Entrada's decision from the start to conduct a controlled study demonstrates faith in its technology. A multiple ascending dose structure will evaluate dosing regimens ranging from 6 mg/kg to 18 mg/kg, with an emphasis on safety and drug uptake. This trial structure will determine the optimal dose and provide early insight into how Entrada's delivery platform translates into meaningful dystrophin production. If the drug demonstrates robust exon skipping at clinically relevant levels, it may differentiate itself from existing PMO-based therapies that have shown low efficacy and inconsistent benefit.

Its global design also positions Entrada for a wider regulatory pathway outside of the UK. It has completed a Phase 1 safety study in healthy volunteers in which ENTR-601-44 was well-tolerated without serious adverse events or safety signals. Though that somewhat validated the drug's pharmacokinetic profile, the upcoming trial will be the first to assess the functional impact in live DMD patients. With few exon 44 mutation treatment options available, even modest dystrophin restoration improvements could be a reason enough to accelerate pathways in the United States and Europe.

MHRA clearance reflects Entrada's rapid clinical progress, but it also sets expectations high. We will need to closely monitor early signals of efficacy, as the absence of meaningful dystrophin restoration would weaken the broader investment thesis surrounding EEV technology. However, a strong result from the trial may not only position ENTR-601-44 as a key player in DMD but also establish the company's platform for future intracellular genetic therapies.

Scientific and Execution Risks

Although Entrada Therapeutics presents a compelling case for its intracellular delivery technology, scientifically and operationally significant risks remain. The company's Endosomal Escape Vehicle platform is based on the premise that its cyclic peptide motif facilitates the escape of oligonucleotides from endosomes into the cytoplasm for further therapeutic action. The problem is that endosomal escape remains a barrier to drug development, and while preclinical data indicates improved uptake, true clinical efficacy in humans is far from certain. Many companies pursued enhanced oligonucleotide delivery strategies, but uptake improvements did not necessarily translate into functional benefits. Even with higher intracellular drug concentrations, Entrada still needs to demonstrate sufficient dystrophin production in Duchenne muscular dystrophy patients to enable real-world strength and mobility gains.

A further scientific risk is the inherent limitation of exon-skipping therapies. Although partial dystrophin production can be restored using this approach, previous attempts with phosphorodiamidate morpholino oligomers have yielded a low quantity and functionally incomplete protein. As an example, Sarepta Therapeutics' (SRPT) approved exon-skipping drugs show some dystrophin restoration but little clinical impact, raising questions about whether these therapies affect disease progression. Entrada's EEV platform improves uptake but does not fundamentally change the biology of exon skipping. If the produced dystrophin is not sufficient to restore muscle integrity, then improved delivery may not be clinically significant.

Beyond the scientific hurdles, Entrada faces execution risks with its trial design and regulatory strategy. With its ELEVATE-44-201 trial, the company is conducting its first real-world EEV technology test in patients - and the data will be scrutinized rigorously with a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design. If results show only modest improvements in dystrophin levels or no correlation with functional benefit, the stock could suffer sharp downsides. I would add that although the company has regulatory clearance in the UK, entry into the U.S. and other major markets may pose additional hurdles if early data fails to meet the bar for accelerated approval pathways.

Valuation and Market Analysis

Entrada trades at a 50% discount to the sector median P/B of 2.25, which means investors are paying little premium to its platform technology, intellectual property, and future revenue potential. A P/B ratio near 1 for a pre-commercial biotech means the market values the pipeline just above its tangible assets - as if its potential has almost no intrinsic value. That compares to the wider biotech sector, where such promising early-stage companies generally command premiums based on platform potential only. If Entrada can demonstrate early success with its clinical programs - ENTR-601-44 - the market may need to rethink its platform beyond cash reserves.

The total addressable market for Duchenne muscular dystrophy provides context. New therapeutic entrants and expanded treatment options could drive a global DMD market of $10 billion annually by 2030, according to current estimates. If Entrada takes even a 10% market share in this niche, that translates to over $1 billion in annual revenue - a big sum for a company valued at levels that suggest little faith in commercial success. More broadly, if the EEV platform opens up new oligonucleotide applications beyond DMD, the upside may even reach multi-billion-dollar markets.

Conclusion and Outlook

Entrada Therapeutics now faces execution decisions that will determine whether it remains an undervalued niche player or becomes a recognized leader in intracellular drug delivery. This pre-commercial biotech has an attractive balance sheet that extends its runway into 2027. Such financial power enables it to validate its Endosomal Escape Vehicle platform - an approach to overcoming one of the most persistent barriers in oligonucleotide-based medicine. If it achieves meaningful dystrophin restoration in its ELEVATE-44-201 trial, it would establish ENTR-601-44 as a competitive therapy for Duchenne muscular dystrophy and also confirm the broad applicability of its EEV technology in multiple disease areas.

data.title: Boston Pizza: More Than Ever, Same-Store Sales Matter For Dividend Sustainability
data.description:
Note: All monetary values ​​in this article are represented in US dollars, except where expressly noted in the analysis.

Delving Deeper into Value Promotions: Is it Generating Traffic?

"A quick lunch doesn't have to mean fast food." That's how Boston Pizza (OTC:BPZZF) promoted its value platform during the fourth quarter of last year. Don't look twice! It's a clear attempt to generate incremental sales from guests who are dissatisfied with the value-per-dollar ratio of fast-food restaurants.

I'm not going to lie. Despite being at least half a year behind, Boston Pizza managed to deliver its best quarter of the year. Sure, the results aren't as impressive as when Chili's (EAT) launched its much-talked-about '3-For-Me' with the beloved Big Smasher at the lower end of the price range, but the promotion certainly made the desired difference.

It's a simple, very Applebee-ish (DIN) deal. An entrée that can range from four sandwich options, three lunch-sized pastas, three lunch-sized salads, three small pizza options (not including the pizzas of the day), a side (fries or soup) and a fountain drink. It's not deja vu. I know you've seen other local chains like BJ's Restaurants (BJRI) and their $13 Pizookie Meal Deal with dessert (which should work out to about CAD$18.50) offering desirable lunchtime deals.

At the end of the day, these CAD$15 deals are on par with the $10 deals south of the border, so I like to compare them to Applebee's Really Big Meal Deal. This one does everything Boston Pizza does, but also offers unlimited fries and a chicken sandwich that's guaranteed to be the biggest on the market.

But unlike Applebee's, which claims that its new Big Cluckin' is the biggest fried chicken sandwich on the market and even guarantees an extra chicken breast at no extra cost to guests who bring a larger sandwich, Boston Pizza is all about convenience. If your food doesn't arrive within twenty minutes, your next lunch at BP will be free! It's an interesting risk reversal that helps reduce the guest's perception of risk. After all, this is a precise lunchtime during work hours; if your food takes too long, at least you'll have a free lunch.

It's a classic 'Better Value Than Fast-Food'!

Either way, it’s working for them. There’s no denying that. You can see that by noticing that same-store sales returned to low-single-digit growth during the quarter after some erratic comparisons that ranged from very low-single-digit growth year-over-year to low-single-digit declines year-over-year.

Here’s how it’s been performing over the past few periods, as well as some comparisons with other full-service restaurants:

Note that after the CAD$15 Meal Deal was introduced in November, same-store sales grew 3.4% year-over-year during the fourth quarter. This came after the company reported same-store sales falling 0.6% year-over-year during the third quarter. This means that Boston Pizza has indeed seen a sequential recovery, even though it faces relatively tough comparisons. These comparisons should become easier starting next quarter, as it will be facing historically weak first-quarter comparisons.

Management itself noted that most of the same-store sales growth came from menu price increases. Therefore, we can see that:

  1. The CAD$15 Meal Deal played a key role in increasing the mix which, in conjunction with the price increases, has caused the average check per guest to grow to the point where it offsets the slower traffic; or
  2. The value promotion contained the decline in traffic, which less strongly offset the growth in the average check caused by the increase in menu prices.

Of course, both things may have happened to different degrees. However, it seems like common sense that the price increase was the biggest driver of same-store sales, despite the '$15 Meal Deal' coming into play.

For Q1, in addition to the value platform, Boston Pizza has added Pasta Tuesday. This offering is designed to bring the restaurant some life on typically slower days. For just CAD$11.99 (approximately $8.45), guests can create their own custom pastas. But that’s not all. For CAD$15.99 (approximately $11.25), an upsell lever can be pulled, and more elaborate pastas can be added.

I expect them to continue growing in the low single digits year-over-year for the first quarter of FY 2025 as Boston Pizza rolls out its value promotions and faces easier comparisons.

Despite Higher Sales, Keep an Eye on Growth!

The Boston Pizza Royalty Fund currently has 372 restaurants in its pool. This represents a drop compared to the 377 restaurants at the end of FY 2023. Look how curious! These restaurants generated system-wide sales of CAD$931.7 million during all of FY 2024. Note that this represents a 0.65% year-over-year increase as existing restaurants began to offset the closures of the five underperforming restaurants. AUVs are currently at CAD$2.5 million, up approximately 2% year-over-year from CAD$2.45 million last year.

This represented revenues for the fund of approximately CAD$37.2 million for the full FY 2024, growing approximately 0.81% year over year. Converting this to net income, the fund generated approximately CAD$31.9 million, growing 7.5% year over year.
